secsa__001_a.text:
Scientific abstract
Background
Vulval Lichen Sclerosus (VLS) is a chronic inflammatory skin disease that affects the vulva and anogenital region
(1). Its prevalence is up to 3% (2,3), with peak onset in prepubertal girls and postmenopausal women. Symptoms
include itching and pain, with flat, porcelain-white lesions. Scarring and loss of vulval architecture can develop in up
to 79% of cases (4). Dyspareunia caused by fourchette fissuring and erosions and psychological impact further
complicate the condition and can have a significant impact on quality of life including intimacy and physical activity.
First-line treatment includes topical corticosteroids (TCS) which are effective during the acute and maintenance
phases but can be insufficient in managing scarring and structural changes. Other treatments have limited efficacy
evidence. New surgical options including autologous fat transfer (AFG) and platelet-rich plasma (PRP) have shown
the potential to improve VLS symptoms and VLS-related scarring and loss of architecture but lack robust prospective
clinical evidence. This study aims to evaluate the feasibility of running a definitive trial investigating these treatments
in VLS.
Aims

secsa__001_b.text:
The primary feasibility outcome will be the proportion of eligible patients willing to be randomised. We will also
assess the adherence to the trial protocol, the feasibility of setting up other treatment centres across the UK, running
a multicentre trial and the acceptability of the treatments to patients and clinicians. We will collect preliminary clinical
efficacy and safety data to identify the most suitable outcome measures and calculate the sample size required for
an adequately powered future multicentre trial.
Methods
We propose a single-blind (blinded assessors) randomised controlled feasibility study with a 1:1:1 randomisation into
three treatment arms: TCS only (Standard of Care – SoC), AFG + SoC and PRP + SoC. Patients in two surgical
arms will undergo a procedure under general anaesthesia where AFG and PRP will be collected and either AFG or
PRP will be injected into the vulval area. Patients will be blinded to the injection they receive. A pragmatic approach
of allowing patients to continue a potent TCS which they are known to tolerate will be used. Patients with poorly
controlled disease will be reviewed by a Consultant Dermatologist. We plan to recruit up to 75 pat

secsa__001_c.text:
ients. The inclusion
criteria are adults (>18 years), female sex, patients with expert-diagnosed clinical and/or histological diagnosis of
VLS, able to give informed consent and communicate in English, and VLS-related structural changes. Patients will
be followed up over the phone during the immediate postoperative period. Follow-up visits will take place at Royal
Free Hospital at 3 and 6 months.

secpesor__001_a.text:
Plain English summary
Background
Vulval lichen sclerosus (VLS) is an itchy condition affecting the skin around the entrance to the vagina (vulva). In
severe cases, it causes scarring and fusion of parts of the vulva, leading to painful skin tears, difficulty in having sex
and going to the toilet. VLS affects women of any age but is most diagnosed before puberty and after menopause.
Treatment includes lifelong use of strong corticosteroid creams, which are effective for most women. For most
patients the condition is controlled by this treatment but some experience ongoing symptoms, scarring and skin
thickening. Recent studies have introduced two potential surgical treatments: fat grafting and platelet-rich plasma
which may improve VLS symptoms and reduce scarring. Fat grafting involves collecting fat from the patient’s own
body and injecting it into the vulva. Platelet-rich plasma is obtained from the patient's blood, prepared in a special
way, and then injected into the vulva.
Aims
We want to carry out a feasibility (pilot) study in which patients will either undergo one of these new treatments or
use corticosteroid cream only to check if these treatments are acceptable and how to
secpesor__001_a.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

secpesor__001_b.text:
best measure their
effectiveness. We also want to know if it is possible to perform a larger trial in multiple hospitals in the future to find
out if these treatments work.
Design and methods
This study will involve up to 75 women with VLS. They will be allocated randomly and equally into three groups: fat
grafting, platelet-rich plasma and corticosteroid cream only. Patients in the surgical groups will undergo one surgery
and will continue using the corticosteroid cream. Participants will complete questionnaires about their symptoms,
quality of life and sexual function. We will assess improvements by analysing photographs of the vulva and
measuring the thickness of the vulval skin using specialised equipment. Patients will be followed up for six months.
Patient and public involvement
Our patient representative, a woman with VLS, has contributed to the study design. We also conducted an online
survey involving 809 women with VLS to shape the study. The patient representative will remain on the trial
committee and participate in decision-making throughout the study. We established a patient advisory group of
women with VLS to gather their ongoing opinions and ensure our research ali
secpesor__001_b.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

secpesor__001_c.text:
gns with patients’ priorities.
Dissemination
The results of this study will be used to determine the design of a large trial which will allow us to check if the new
treatments work and whether they can be offered to patients across the UK. We will create an online platform where
the results will be disseminated. The results will be shared through patient support groups, social media, medical
conferences and academic journals.
secpesor__001_c.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

Changes from Stage 1
1. Please provide information on Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA)
correspondence in relation to the proposed therapies: PlatEleT-rich plasma and Autologous lipotransfer.
Autologous fat grafting (AFG); Has been used in clinical practice by Professor Peter Butler since 2001. Professor
Peter Butler met with the Medicines and Healthcare Products Regulatory Agency (MHRA) in 2017 and after
discussion with the MHRA was informed that this procedure is considered under the regulations as resizing and not
a cell therapy and therefore does not require MHRA approval as it has no manipulation other than centrifugation as
per standardised practice in the operating room before injection. Minutes from the meeting have been provided as an
attachment (Appendix_MHRA correspondence) This meeting occurred bef
seccfps__001_d.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Changes from Previous Stage

seccfps__001_e.text:
ore its use in a clinical trial.
Platelet Rich Plasma; PRP is used clinically in many conditions including vulval lichen sclerosus (VLS). We met with
David R Jones FRSB FBTS Expert Pharmaco-Toxicologist who communicated with the MHRA on our behalf
regarding this feasibility trial. We were informed that if the PRP used was not altered with calcium chloride or other
additive it is not considered an investigational medicinal product and therefore does not need MHRA approval.
Furthermore, this is a feasibility trial and is not a clinical trial and therefore does not need MHRA approval. The
results of this feasibility trial will inform the design of subsequent a prospective randomised multicentre clinical trial.
Attached is a copy of email correspondence with the MHRA confirming this approach (Appendix_MHRA
correspondence).
2. Provide more details on recruitment and discuss the feasibility of meeting recruitment targets through
only one site.
Potential participants will be recruited from two sites: Royal Free Hospital and Nottingham University Hospitals.
Approximately 500 patients are seen at the Vulval clinic at Royal Free Hospital each year, which includes 380 new
patient referrals wi
seccfps__001_e.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Changes from Previous Stage

seccfps__001_f.text:
th VLS. Additionally, there are 200 VLS patients seen at Nottingham University Hospital each
year. We also have identified two additional patient referral centres: the Department of Dermatology, Southampton
(under Dr Helen Lowery and Dr Alice Plant) and the Department of Dermatology, Norwich (under Dr Karen Gibbon).
There are 350 new VLS patients seen at Southampton each year.
Minimum target recruitment of 3 patients per month has been estimated as outlined in the traffic light system (Table
4). This has been set as a minimum number for the outcome of this feasibility trial with a number of mitigations to
achieve and exceed this minimum recruitment. We plan to recruit a maximum of 25 patients per group which would
result in a total sample size of 75.
We are going to review the recruitment rate during regular Trial Management Group meetings and will consider
identifying additional referral sites if the recruitment rate falls within the red or amber criteria outlined in Table 4.
We are also going to increase awareness of the trial through the VLS online patient support groups, national
dermatology, gynaecology and plastic surgery conferences and collaboration with agencies like the B
seccfps__001_f.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Changes from Previous Stage

seccfps__001_g.text:
ritish Society
for the Study of Vulval Disease (BSSVD) (Please see the Appendix_BSSVD Support Letter) and Wellbeing of
Women (WoW) charity to increase feasibility trial recruitment rates.
3. Discuss what consideration had been given to the impact of travel between London and Nottingham on
participants and recruitment.
Currently, patients are referred to Professor Butler from across the UK for lipotransfer treatment.
For this study, we will offer to reimburse the cost of travel for all eligible patients. We have increased the provision for
the travel cost from Nottingham as well as any other recruitment sites. We estimated this based on an average
transport cost from Nottingham. This is detailed in the SoECAT form.
This will reduce the impact of travel from Nottingham, Norwich, Southampton and other locations on patients and
therefore improve recruitment and retention.
The impact of the distance travelled to the treatment centre on patient recruitment and retention will be one of the
feasibility outcomes of this study.
We will assess the impact of travel on patients and recruitment through questionnaires administered to all eligible
patients regardless of whether they agreed to part
seccfps__001_g.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Changes from Previous Stage

seccfps__001_h.text:
icipate or not. We will therefore be able to monitor if travel is an
issue and mitigate this risk if possible but also use this data to support the design of a future clinical trial.
4. Justify the inclusion of efficacy outcomes in a feasibility study.
The randomised prospective data on the proposed treatments is very poor in the literature. The utility of the
proposed outcome measures is not adequately assessed in this cohort of patients in the literature. Their inclusion
will help us determine whether these measures are appropriately sensitive to change and should be or can be
included in the future trial.
We will assess the acceptability of the proposed outcome assessments to patients and clinicians and decide which
assessment tools should be used in the definitive trial.
The inclusion of efficacy outcomes will also allow us to calculate a sample size for the adequately powered definitive
trial and establish the appropriate follow-up schedule and duration.
5. Consider the inclusions of a sham procedure in the control arm with the aim of strengthening the
evidence obtained from a definitive trial.
We have previously considered including a sham procedure (saline injections under g
seccfps__001_h.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Changes from Previous Stage

seccfps__001_i.text:
eneral anaesthesia) in addition
to topical corticosteroids in the control group of this study.
We have presented the study protocol to members of the British Society for the Study of Vulval Disease (BSSVD)
and discussed the inclusion of a sham procedure. The majority recognised the advantage of the trial design which
includes a sham procedure, but some raised ethical concerns about performing a placebo procedure under general
anaesthesia. Others were concerned about the possibility of exacerbating VLS symptoms by injection of saline into
the disease-affected area. Therefore, we feel this is something we need to explore in this feasibility study.
We have also discussed the inclusion of a sham procedure with our patient groups during our PPI work. The sham
procedure would involve a patient undergoing a procedure under general anaesthesia, harvesting of abdominal fat
with a standard liposuction cannula and 20mls of blood from the antecubital veins which is the source of PRP all of
which would be discarded. All patients who attended the initial focus group (n=18) said that they would not take part
in this study if a sham procedure was included. We posed the same question to a larger gr
seccfps__001_i.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Changes from Previous Stage

seccfps__001_j.text:
oup of 809 patients
through an online questionnaire. Seventy-three percent of respondents said that they would be willing to take part in
the study if a sham procedure was used. The most common reasons for refusing to take part if a sham procedure
was used included pain and fear of worsening symptoms.
Because of the mixed opinions from both patients and clinicians on the inclusion of a sham procedure, we need to
examine the acceptability or lack thereof. This may impact not only our ability to recruit and retain patients in a future
clinical trial but also our ability to achieve the largest potential clinical referral to this potential future trial. We are
concerned that including a sham procedure in this study might affect the recruitment rate before the patients are
referred to our centre and we might not be able to assess if it is a barrier to recruitment. We will add the acceptability
of including a sham procedure in the definitive trial as one of the outcomes of this feasibility trial.
We recognise the scientific value of a sham group inclusion and additionally, there is very limited published evidence
using a sham control versus PRP treatment of VLS. We identified only one ra
seccfps__001_j.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Changes from Previous Stage

seccfps__001_k.text:
ndomised controlled trial which
compared platelet-rich plasma injections (n=19) to saline injections (n=10) (5). The authors used histology,
appearance and patient symptoms as outcome measures. They showed no significant difference in all measures
between groups. There are no trials with sham in AFG and no trials with sham against AFG and PRP.
We will ask patients to complete questionnaires after recruitment into the study and after surgery to assess the
following:
- After randomisation: Would you have agreed to be randomised into this study if a sham procedure
was included? If No, can you please explain why?
- After the surgery: Having been through the surgical procedure and general anaesthesia, would you
have agreed to undergo this process if there was a chance that you would have a saline injection
instead of the treatment? If No, can you please explain why?
The results of these questionnaires, in conjunction with discussions with the patient advisory board, patient
representative and the co-applicants will help us decide whether a sham procedure should be included in the
definitive trial from a patient perspective. We will also approach agencies like the British Society for the
seccfps__001_k.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Changes from Previous Stage

seccfps__001_l.text:
Study of
Vulval Disease to survey clinicians and seek their opinions through formal presentations, focus groups and clinical
acceptability surveys.
6. Provide justification how adherence to protocol and acceptability of treatment to clinicians can be
assessed with RFH as the only study centre and the only centre in the country already providing autologous
fat transfer.
Acceptability
We have already engaged using a focus group with dermatologists and gynaecologists and we will further expand
this work with our collaborating colleagues from Southampton, Nottingham and Norwich to assess the acceptability
of the study protocol and treatments and adherence to protocol as part of our feasibility trial. Using this focus group
feedback, we will develop quantitative questionnaires concerning the key themes around the acceptability or lack
thereof of the future clinical trial and the acceptability of the interventions themselves to the clinical community.
We have presented our trial design proposal to the British Society for the Study of Vulval Disease as well as
discussed clinical acceptability, patient recruitment, retention and sham procedure acceptability. We plan to further
expand on t
seccfps__001_l.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Changes from Previous Stage

seccfps__001_m.text:
his engagement through formal presentations, focus groups and clinical acceptability surveys.
We will collect the data on the acceptability of treatment to clinicians through formal focus groups, clinical
acceptability surveys, academic conference presentations and informal discussions.
Adherence
If patients are found to be non-adherent to the study protocol, we will organise a telephone follow-up with them and
assess the reasons for non-adherence to identify ways to support them and improve treatment adherence. All
patients who withdrew from the study will be asked to complete a feedback survey to help us identify the causes and
solutions to improve patients’ experience.
The trial will be overseen by the Trial Steering Committee and the Independent Data Management Committee. Both
groups will monitor the adherence to treatment and data completeness.
7. The Committee recommended further development of the patient and public involvement (PPI)
component. In particular, additional details about how the PPI representatives would be involved in the
study and how their views would be taken into account were requested. The team were also requested to
justify the inclusion of the PPI member
seccfps__001_m.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Changes from Previous Stage

seccfps__001_n.text:
at only 1% FTE.
Our patient co-applicant, a female with VLS, who is an active participant in online VLS patient support groups will be
a crucial part of this trial. She has been involved in the trial design and planning and has reviewed the plain English
Summary, Patient Information Sheets and Consent Form. She will be a member of the Trial Management Group and
will attend regular meetings to discuss the trial progression. She will be supported in her role as the Patient and
Public Involvement (PPI) lead by an academic clinical fellow and consultant clinical psychologist before, during and
after the meetings to ensure that she has a full understanding of the trial procedures and can make informed
contributions to the discussions. In addition, the agenda for every meeting will have a permanent item for discussion
of the PPI matters. Her FTE commitment has been increased to 5% to reflect the Stage 1 feedback. She will receive
compensation for her time calculated at a daily rate of £120 per meeting and in addition any travel costs incurred.
This has been added to the study budget.
In addition, we will seek to appoint an independent PPI member of the Trial Steering Committee who will
seccfps__001_n.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Changes from Previous Stage

seccfps__001_o.text:
also be
reimbursed for their time.
We have recruited a trial patient advisory group (PAG) consisting of 4 women with VLS from Royal Free Hospital
and Nottingham University Hospital. Patients from Nottingham have a vast experience in PPI work through their
previous work on the PEARLS trial (Proactive against reactive therapy for the prevention of lichen sclerosus
exacerbation and progression of disease – a pragmatic, parallel-group randomised controlled trial with embedded
economic evaluation and process evaluation) (NIHR funded). One of the patients is an active member of the Vulval
Cancer Awareness UK charity. The PAG has already been engaged in the trial design and will be consulted during
the study progression. They will attend regular online focus groups to discuss the trial progression and improve
recruitment if necessary to improve patients’ adherence and experience.
Our PPI members are experts by experience. Their input will ensure that the research outputs of this feasibility study
are aligned with patients’ needs and priorities.
Additionally, all potentially eligible patients will be interviewed to explore the reasons for agreeing or refusing to take
part in the study to f
seccfps__001_o.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Changes from Previous Stage

seccfps__001_p.text:
urther improve the trial design, identify any barriers to recruitment and seek opinions regarding
improving recruitment rates. All patients will be asked about their opinion on a sham procedure after randomisation
and after the surgical procedure to explore whether a sham procedure should be included in the definitive trial.
One of the feasibility aims of this trial will be to identify the primary and secondary outcomes for the definitive trial
and the most suitable outcome assessment tools. Our patient representative, the patient advisory board and the
study participants will be asked about the acceptability of the proposed outcomes assessment tools and the most
suitable outcome measures which should be included in the definitive trial.
8. The Committee requested more information on the prevalence of vulvar fibrosis in Lichen Sclerosus (VLS)
in all populations and how many patients seek treatment through primary and secondary care.
We have added detailed information on the prevalence of vulval fibrosis in VLS in the Background and Rationale
Section.
The prevalence of VLS in the UK is up to 3% but universally accepted as under-reported (2,3). The incidence is
highest in postmenopau
seccfps__001_p.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Changes from Previous Stage

seccfps__001_q.text:
sal women but girls as young as 4-6 years old are affected, comprising 7-15% of cases and
commonly diagnosis is delayed by 1-2 years (6,7).
The prevalence of vulval fibrosis and scarring in VLS is poorly reported but studies have reported rates as high as
64-79% in adult women (2,4) and 27% in prepubertal girls (4).
There is no national register of the number of VLS patients seeking care for VLS or vulval fibrosis through primary
and secondary care.
In this study, one of the eligibility criteria includes the vulval structural changes. We will be assessing the effect of the
proposed treatments not only on fibrosis but also on sexual function, impact on intimate relationships, pain, itching
and quality of life which were prioritised by our original PPI work.
9. The Committee sought clarity on the progression criteria to the full trial, and requested the team consider
the use of the “traffic light” system.
We have adopted the traffic light system as suggested in the Stage 1 feedback. We summarised the proposed traffic
light system including the proportion of eligible participants willing to be randomised, recruitment rate per month,
number of active patient recruitment sites, withdraw
seccfps__001_q.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Changes from Previous Stage

seccfps__001_r.text:
al rate and data completeness in Table 4 (criteria for progression
to the subsequent, definitive trial). We have identified mitigation strategies for each element (Table 5). We will review
these at Trial Management Group meetings and how these issues may impact on a future clinical trial.
10. The Committee requested that the plain English summary (PES) be rewritten to remove the excess
jargon and acronyms and to also include an explanation of vulvar fibrosis in Lichen Sclerosus. Please note
that the word limit for a PES is 450 words.
The Plain English Summary (PES) has been reviewed by our patient representative and four members of the PAG.
As a result, all jargon and abbreviations have been removed and a clearer explanation of the vulval anatomy, VLS
signs and symptoms and their impact on the patients’ life have been added.
We also included a simple explanation of vulval fibrosis in VLS. All patients agreed that the updated PES is easy to
understand and includes the most important information about the trial design.
seccfps__001_r.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Changes from Previous Stage

1. Background and Rationale
What is the problem being addressed?
Vulval Lichen Sclerosus (VLS) is a chronic inflammatory skin disease affecting the vulva and anogenital region (1).
The prevalence of VLS in the UK is up to 3% of adult women (2,3), and girls as young as 4-6 years are also affected
(7-15% of cases) (6,7).
VLS aetiology is unknown but is believed to be a combination of genetic susceptibility and environmental factors
triggering an autoimmune reaction which leads to inflammation, tissue injury, dysregulation of fibroblast and collagen
metabolism (1).
VLS symptoms include itching, burning and pain. Typical lesions are flat, porcelain-white spots with erythema,
ecchymosis, hyperkeratosis, and excoriations due to itching (1). Diagnosis is clinically based and supplemented by
biopsy, in particular when neoplastic lesions are suspected (1). VLS has a 4-6.7% risk of vulval squamous cell
carcinoma (8,9).
Like the untreated course in adults, failure to treat prepubertal VLS patients leads to progressive scarring with loss of
archi

secdrp__001_c.text:
tecture in up to 75% of adolescent cases (6). VLS symptoms in girls may also differ from the adult presentation
with girls being more likely to have urinary or bowel symptoms and vulval purpura at first presentation (4).
Forty percent of women of reproductive age may be asymptomatic and are commonly misdiagnosed (9,10). Pruritus,
which is the most reported VLS symptom, is more pronounced in hypoestrogenic states and could be the reason
why women in the reproductive years experience milder symptoms, possibly contributing to the delay in diagnosis
(9). The time from the reported onset of symptoms until diagnosis is up to five years (4,10). Many cases present late
and are misdiagnosed as candida or other mild vulval disorders (10).
There is no cure for VLS and treatments are limited (1). First-line treatment involves lifelong topical corticosteroids
(TCS) which are effective in reducing inflammation, development of fibrosis and change in architecture (11).
Previous studies have shown that less than 67% of patients comply with treatment (12).
A meta-analysis found that TCS are effective in the acute and maintenance phases but are inadequate for reversing
the scarring and loss of vulval

secdrp__001_d.text:
architecture once it has already occurred (13). The prevalence of vulval fibrosis and
scarring in VLS is poorly reported but studies have reported rates as high as 64-79% in adult women (2,4) and 27%
in prepubertal girls (4). A delay in diagnosis of 2 years or less is associated with less scarring on presentation
suggesting that early diagnosis may prevent scarring in some cases (4). Other treatments have limited efficacy
evidence (13).
VLS commonly affects the fourchette, labia minora and majora, clitoral hood, perineum and perianal area.
Dyspareunia caused by fourchette fissuring, erosions and fissures impacts sexual functioning and intimate
relationships (14).
Two surgical treatments that might be effective in reversing VLS-related inflammation, fissuring and fibrosis,
autologous fat transfer (AFG) and platelet-rich plasma (PRP), have limited evidence of clinical efficacy. There are no
high-quality randomised controlled trials (RCTs) comparing these options against each other and standard care.
Why is this research important in terms of improving the health and/or wellbeing of the public and/or to
patients and health care services?
Impact of VLS
The European Commission has ackn

secdrp__001_e.text:
owledged VLS as a high-burden, under-researched condition, highlighting
insufficient funding relative to the disease burden (15). VLS causes sexual dysfunction manifesting as dyspareunia,
vulvodynia, difficulty achieving orgasm, and loss of sexual interest (14,16). A 2015 British Association of
Dermatologists vulval health survey involving 325 women revealed substantial daily life disruptions, including
difficulties in walking (38%) and sleeping (36%). Sexual function was impaired in 84%, 79% experienced strain on
personal relationships, 89% reported negative effects on emotional wellbeing with 22% reporting thoughts of suicide
or self-harm. Work capacity is diminished in 39% (17).
Improving future health services and VLS care
This study addresses several of the Top 10 priorities of the VLS Priority Setting Partnership (18). If feasibility is
shown, the subsequent RCT will aim to fill the evidence gaps in the surgical management of functional and structural
architectural changes, symptom reduction and improvement of quality of life (QoL). High-quality prospective
evidence is required to develop best practice guidelines to standardise treatment delivery.
The Royal Free Hospital is t

secdrp__001_f.text:
he only NHS centre providing AFG for VLS, with one centre in Nottingham having done
so in the past. There is a sporadic provision of AFG and PRP privately without an adequate evidence base. There is
no provision of PRP for VLS in the NHS. This study will include Nottingham as a Patient Identification Centre (PIC)
and will explore the feasibility of a second treatment centre.
Review of existing evidence – How does the existing literature support this proposal?
Systematic reviews of clinical efficacy
We conducted a systematic review of PRP and AFG as VLS treatments, identifying 15 studies of mostly poor quality
(level 3-4 evidence). These studies demonstrated improvement in VLS signs, symptoms, QoL and sexual function.
One RCT (5), reported as an abstract, assessed PRP in VLS, finding no significant difference between the
experimental and control groups. One RCT (19) reported the efficacy of PRP mixed with AFG and found significant
improvements in fissures, itching, dyspareunia, QoL and inflammation. We found, as others did, that heterogeneity of
outcome measures hindered meaningful analysis (13). No trials compared PRP vs AFG vs TCS alone.
Trial register
Having searched clinical tri

secdrp__001_g.text:
al databases, we identified two RTCs evaluating the effectiveness of PRP in VLS
(20,21)(Table 1). There are no currently ongoing studies evaluating AFG in VLS.
Table 1. RCT evaluating PRP in VLS.
Pilot study
We carried out two studies; a retrospective open cohort study and a prospective open cohort study.
The retrospective cohort study evaluating the effectiveness of AFG in VLS (n=59) found significant improvement in
the visual analogue scale for itching and a significant decrease in sexual distress.
The prospective cohort study (n=33) of patients treated with AFG (16). We found that AFG has a potential anti-
inflammatory and anti-fibrotic effect (Figure 1).
Figure 1. Results of the prospective cohort study.
Validation of additional outcome measures
In this trial, two assessment methods we have previously validated will be used: the Vulvar Architecture Severity
Scale (22) and 3D Volumetric Imaging (23).
Patient and Public Involvement
In line with NIHR Patient and Public Involvement resources, the NIHR Collaboration for Leadership in Applied Health
Research Toolkit and utilising the GRIPP2-SF framework (24) Patient and Public Involvement (PPI) has been
present throughout the trial d

secdrp__001_h.text:
esign and used as a keystone in this feasibility trial.
We worked with our patient co-applicant and a PPI focus group regularly to identify key themes:
 Is present treatment adequate?
 Is this trial necessary and relevant?
 Could we include a sham?
 Barriers to entry and long-term engagement?
 What are the key outcomes we should measure?
The methods used included:
 One-to-one discussions with our patient representative and members of the patient advisory group (PAG)
 Focus group and one-to-one interviews with 18 VLS patients
 Online survey of the trial proposal and preferred outcome measures completed by 809 VLS patients
 Small focus groups and one-to-one interviews with our PPI co-applicant and members of the PAG in
response to NIHR Stage 1 feedback
The initial PPI stage included 18 patients. Patients were invited to a focus group to discuss trial design, the proposed
procedures, the sham procedure, and potential outcome measures. The outcomes tested with the group were
derived from the VLS outcome domains (25). Results are summarised in Figure 2.
Figure 2. PPI focus group results.
The online survey was designed in collaboration with our trial consultant clinical psycholo

secdrp__001_i.text:
gist and the patient co-
applicant based on the focus group outcomes. It was disseminated on national and international VLS patient forums
to learn from a wider international group of VLS patients. It was completed by 809 women. The results are
summarised in Figure 3.
Figure 3. PPI online survey results.
The survey results highlighted the need for the study and the lack of effective treatments for VLS-related fibrosis.
We are committed to involving the patients at all stages of the PETALS trial. We have involved our PPI Lead from
the early stages of the trial and used her advice to shape the trial design. We also created a PAG which consists of
four women with VLS to ensure that patients’ views shape the progression of the PETALS trial. One of the members
of our PAG is a founder of the Lichen Sclerosus and Vulval Cancer Awareness website
(https://www.lsvcukawareness.co.uk) with experience in advocating for women with VLS and vulval cancer and
raising awareness of these conditions. The PAG will advise us on the trial throughout its lifecycle through regular
online meetings, questionnaires, results dissemination and creation of the online platform to increase awareness of
the trial a

secdrp__001_j.text:
nd the proposed treatments.
The Plain English Summary has been reviewed by our patient representative and four members of the PAG. All
jargon has been removed and a clearer explanation of the vulval anatomy, VLS signs and symptoms and their
impact on patients’ lives have been added. All patients agreed that the updated summary is easy to understand.
2. Aims and objectives
What is the research question / aims and objectives?
Our feasibility trial will aim to establish if the progression to the definitive trial is possible. This will be achieved by
answering the following questions:
1. What proportion of eligible patients are willing to be randomised?
2. What is the average number of patients we can recruit per month and the number of patients we can recruit
in total?
3. What is the rate of withdrawal from the study, data collection completeness and the impact of the distance
travelled to the treatment centre on the patient retention rate?
4. Are the treatments acceptable to the patients and clinicians?
5. What is the adherence to the trial protocol and adequate timing of outcome assessments?
6. What is the ideal number of centres required nationally? Can a second treatment centre be

secdrp__001_k.text:
set up in
Nottingham?
7. What is the optimal measure of efficacy for a multicentre RCT?
8. What is the rate of adverse events?
9. Should a sham procedure be included in the control group in the definitive trial? Is it acceptable to the
patients and clinicians?
10. What is the feasibility of collecting quantitative and health economics data and analysis of cost-
effectiveness?
11. What is the best way to disseminate the results among clinicians and raise awareness of the study and the
proposed interventions?
12. How to disseminate the results among the patients and increase awareness of the study and the proposed
procedures? What online platform should be used for communicating with patients?
3. Detailed Research Plan and Methods
Study design
This will be a single-blind (blinded assessors) randomised controlled feasibility trial (Please see
Appendix_CONSORT flow diagram). Following confirmation of eligibility and written informed consent, participants
will be randomised using a centralised online randomisation system (https://sealedenvelope.com/) to ensure
randomisation concealment and minimise bias. Randomisation and allocation will be overseen by the trial
coordinator who will no

secdrp__001_l.text:
t be involved in the recruitment, treatment or assessment. All patients will be started on a
standardised TCS (Standard of care - ‘SoC’) and vulval care regimen at the baseline visit after randomisation.
Randomisation will be 1:1:1 to three treatment arms:
1. SoC alone
2. AFG + SoC
3. PRP + SoC
The number of patients recruited per month will be one of the feasibility outcomes. We plan to recruit a maximum of
75 patients. We outlined the target recruitment per month in Table 4 and the Green criteria has been set as at least 3
patients per month.
Patients in two surgical arms will undergo a procedure under general anaesthesia where AFG and PRP are collected
and either AFG or PRP is injected into the vulval area. Patients will be blinded to the injection they receive.
We plan to use unmodified PRP and AFG prepared to a standard protocol injected intraoperatively with the MHRA
who informed us that if used this way, PRP and AFG are acceptable for clinical use and are not Investigational
Medicinal Products (IMP).
A pragmatic approach of allowing patients to continue a potent TCS which they are known to tolerate will be used, as
agreed with dermatology (RS). A maintenance regimen of twice

secdrp__001_m.text:
weekly (non-consecutive days) will occur. If patients
experience a flare-up of VLS signs and symptoms an increase will be advised (TCS daily for seven days) as
standard practice.
We will offer a Consultant Dermatologist review as adjuvant management if clinically indicated by the research team
to manage patients whose disease is poorly controlled.
All patients will be followed-up for 6 months.
Health technologies being assessed
AFG is a minimally invasive surgical procedure. Adipose tissue is harvested from the abdomen with a liposuction
cannula, centrifuged and injected into VLS-affected areas.
PRP is obtained from a blood sample which is centrifuged to isolate platelet-rich fraction and injected into the VLS-
affected areas.
Difference between current and planned pathways
Patients in the control arm will continue SoC (standardised TCS regimen), the same as the current NHS SoC. The
interventions include AFG and PRP. AFG is a routine practice at Royal Free Hospital for VLS patients but is not
common practice nationally. PRP is currently not offered for VLS in the NHS.
Setting
Eligible patients will be identified at Royal Free, Nottingham, Southampton and Norwich. The external cent

secdrp__001_n.text:
res will
refer patients to Royal Free Hospital where they will be randomised, treated and followed-up. To reduce the risk of
slow study progression due to NHS waiting times, patients will be operated through a waiting list initiative which will
be factored in the excess treatment costs. To optimise the recruitment rate, Nottingham Hospital will act as a second
Patient Identification Centre (PIC) and refer patients for treatment and follow-up at Royal Free Hospital. We will also
receive referrals from Southampton through Dr Helen Lowery and Dr Alice Plant (Dermatology) and from Norwich
through Dr Karen Gibbon (Dermatology).
We will increase awareness of the trial among patients through our activity on the online VLS patient support groups.
We will continue to collaborate with female vulval health agencies like the British Society for the Study of Vulval
Disease (BSSVD) (Please see the Appendix_BSSVD Support Letter) and the Wellbeing of Women (WoW) to
increase awareness of the study among healthcare professionals.
Summary of patients as research participants
Target population
We consulted the NIHR INCLUDE Guidelines (26) to ensure the eligibility criteria allow the inclusion of under

secdrp__001_o.text:
served
patients. We will record age, ethnicity, educational level, employment status and other indicators of underserved
patients.
Inclusion criteria
 Adults (>18 years)
 Female sex
Patients who live in the UK

 Patients with expert-diagnosed clinical and/or histological diagnosis of VLS
 Able to give informed consent
 Able to communicate in English
 Structural changes secondary to VLS
Exclusion criteria
 Active malignancy or previous diagnosis of intraepithelial neoplasia/carcinoma of the vulva
 Previous vulval AFG or PRP or vulval surgery
 Body mass index <18.5 or >35.0
 Contraindications to general anaesthetic
 Active infection that precludes fat harvest, AFG or PRP injection
 Pregnancy, breastfeeding or planning for pregnancy during the study period
 Connective tissue and autoimmune diseases and diseases which affect the vulva
Screening, eligibility assessment and consent
Trained members of the research team and clinical collaborators on the Delegation of responsibility log at Royal
Free Hospital, Nottingham University Hospital, Southampton and Norwich will identify eligible participants attending
Dermatology and Vulval clinics and inform them about the trial. All

secdrp__001_p.text:
potentially eligible patients who express initial
interest in taking part will be given Patient Information Sheets and Consent Forms. This will be followed by a phone
call from a member of the research team to discuss the study details and confirm their interest and eligibility.
If eligible and willing to take part, potential participants will be invited to attend a clinic appointment at Royal Free
Hospital. All patients will be given time to ask questions before they provide written informed consent witnessed by a
member of the study team. They will then be randomised into one of the trial arms and undergo the baseline
assessments.
A copy of the signed consent form will be given to the patient, another placed in the patient’s notes, and the
researchers will retain a further copy.
If patients do not consent to take part in the study, they will be asked to complete a feedback form to help us
understand barriers to recruitment.
Patients randomised to the surgical arms (PRP or AFG) will undergo additional baseline assessments on the day of
surgery.
Follow-up
Participants will be followed-up at Royal Free Hospital. They will be able to discuss any urgent queries with a trained
clinica

secdrp__001_q.text:
l research team member via the PETALS trial helpline. We will allocate a member of staff to reply to patients’
queries. This service will be available from Monday to Friday and will help us to work closely with our patients.
Participants in the AFG and PRP arms will be contacted by a research team member via telephone 7 and 14 days
postoperatively to monitor for any complications and offer advice if necessary. All patients will attend follow-up visits
at 3 months and 6 months. Patients will be reimbursed for travel. Each follow-up visit will last approximately one
hour. A summary of assessments made at each visit is presented below in Table 2.
All patients will be on topical corticosteroids and the frequency of use will be monitored through patient diaries and
assessed during the follow-up. We will explore the use of electronic reminders and patient diaries to assess which
format works best for patients and maximises data completeness. This will be explored with the PPI lead and PAG
members.
Table 2. A summary of assessments made at each visit.
Data collection
A formal Data Management Plan will be constructed to describe the procedures involved in the data management
activities and

secdrp__001_r.text:
processes for the study so that it is managed following the Good Clinical Practice guidelines, standard
operating procedures (SOPs), appropriate regulatory requirements and the study protocol.
Data collected during the trial is summarised in Table 3 below. Source data will be in the form of, but not linked to,
patient medical records and study-specific care report forms. This will be stored at Royal Free Hospital as encrypted
and anonymised password-protected files stored on NHS computers. Trial-specific data will be entered into the
database by a dedicated member of staff at Royal Free Hospital and this will be verified by the Trial Manager. All
paper forms will be stored in a locked cabinet in a designated office at Royal Free Hospital and will only be accessed
by authorised trial personnel.
Screening logs will be kept at each recruitment site and will be sent to the Royal Free Hospital periodically to
tabulate the information.
The software model for the data entry and monitoring will be REDCap, which is a secure, web-based platform
providing an interface for validated data capture, audit trails for tracking data manipulation and export procedures.
During the trial, Reporting Da

secdrp__001_s.text:
ta Extracts will be provided for safety reporting, independent Trial Steering Committee
meetings, interim and final analysis, and other reporting periods as outlined in the Statistical Analysis Plan. Only
pseudo-anonymised data will be shared under the terms of the consent forms and will be available to users under a
data-sharing request. After analysis, all study data will be stored and archived according to the Sponsor’s SOPs for
up to 10 years.
Table 3. Summary of data collected during this feasibility study.
Statistical Analysis Plan
A CONSORT diagram will be produced to describe the flow of patients.
All feasibility outcomes will be reported using descriptive statistics. Continuous endpoints will be summarised for
baseline characteristics using means and standard deviations (medians and interquartile ranges) per arm.
Categorical and binary variables will be summarised with frequencies and percentages per arm.
Surgical complications will be reported using the Clavien Dindo scale with descriptive statistics. Adverse events will
be tabulated by treatment arm with frequencies and percentages.
The efficacy endpoints will be analysed with linear mixed models to provide the differenc

secdrp__001_t.text:
es in means with 95%
confidence intervals between the surgical and control arms at the two follow-up time points, adjusted for baseline.
Summaries of the regular data reviews will be shared with the oversight committees.
Health Economics Analysis
The feasibility study will aim to plan the economic evaluation within the definitive RCT, aiming to estimate the cost
and the potential economic benefit for the NHS, personal and social services. We will explore the feasibility of
collecting data to assess the costs for participants and families from a societal perspective.
Within the feasibility study, we will:
 Identify the main cost components of each surgical intervention (equipment, materials, staff cost)
 Identify the potential costs of treating side effects following surgical interventions
 Collaborate with the PPI group to adapt the Client Service Receipt Inventory for the study to collect data on
participants’ health and social care resources
 Identify the main costs for participants and families (e.g., productivity losses)
 Assess participants’ Health-related Quality of life and calculate Quality-Adjusted Life Years (QALYs)
 Perform a literature search to identify economic

secdrp__001_u.text:
studies and evidence available on similar interventions (e.g.,
to capture data that might be needed in addition to those collected within the feasibility trial)
 Design a decision model (e.g., Markov model) using data from the feasibility study to describe the
participants’ pathway in the three treatment arms and to model costs and effects beyond trial based on up-
to-date UK sources, published literature and clinicians and PPI representatives’ opinions
In the definitive RCT, a model-based approach will be used to assess cost-effectiveness.
We will report descriptive statistics of EQ-5D-5L index values, QALYs, cost of surgical interventions and healthcare
resource use per participant per group. We will perform a preliminary analysis of the cost-utility of surgical
interventions against control arms for VLS to inform the definitive trial. We will report the mean incremental cost per
QALY gained.
Measurement of outcomes
Feasibility outcomes
1. The proportion of eligible patients willing to be randomised (the primary feasibility outcome).
2. The average number of patients recruited per month and the total number of recruited patients.
3. Rate of withdrawal from the study, data collec

secdrp__001_v.text:
tion completeness and the impact of the distance travelled to
the treatment centre on patient retention rate.
4. Acceptability of the proposed treatments to patients and clinicians.
5. Adherence to the trial protocol and adequate timing of outcome assessments.
6. Feasibility of setting up a second treatment centre in Nottingham and evaluation of the number of centres
required nationally to run a multicentre trial.
7. The appropriate outcome assessments and their timing. Investigated measures will include:
a. Patient-reported signs and symptoms – Vulvovaginal Symptom Questionnaire (VSQ), Visual
assessment score (VAS) for soreness, itching, burning, dyspareunia, The Female Genital Self-
Image Scale (FGSIS), Clinical Lichen Sclerosus Score (CLISSCO) subjective part, The Vulvar Pain
Assessment Questionnaire (VPAQ), Pain Anxiety Symptom Scale Short Form 20 (PASS-20),
Genital Appearance Assessment Scale (GAAS)
b. Patient-reported sexual function - Female sexual function index (FSFI), Female Sexual Distress
Scale (FSDS)
c. Patient-reported psychological status and QoL questionnaires - Vulvar quality of life index (VQLI),
Dermatology Life Quality Index (DLQI), Skindex-16, Short Form Survey

secdrp__001_w.text:
12 (SF-12), Hospital Anxiety
and Depression Scale (HADS), Relationship Assessment Scale (RAS), Clinician-reported outcomes
– Vulvar Architecture Severity Scale (VASS), CLISSCO objective part, Clitoral phimosis, Interlabial
sulci involvement, Vulvar introitus narrowing (CIV) score
d. Imaging - 3D Vectra h1 & 2D photography, measurement of microcirculation by laser speckle
contrast imaging and visual capillary microscope (HVC)
e. Skin fibrosis - FibroMeter, cutometer, durometer, high-frequency ultrasound
8. Rate of adverse events.
9. Acceptability to include a sham intervention to patients and clinicians in a future randomised prospective
trial.
10. Feasibility of collecting health economics data and preliminary estimates of cost-effectiveness using:
 Healthcare resource utilisation – Client Service Receipt Inventory (CSRI)
 Health-related QoL – EQ-5D-5L
 Impairments in work and activities - Work Productivity and Activity Impairment (WPAI)
11. The most appropriate way to communicate with patients to disseminate the results of the study and increase
awareness of the proposed treatments and the definitive trial.
12. The most appropriate way to communicate with clinicians to dissemi

secdrp__001_x.text:
nate the results of the study and
increase awareness of the proposed treatments and the definitive trial.
All assessment methods are non-invasive. All questionnaires used in this study have been previously validated and
are widely used in trials investigating VLS. We have reviewed all the questionnaires with our psychology co-applicant
(EH) to assess their suitability for inclusion.
A search of the COMET database revealed a lack of finalised VLS core outcome set. However, core domains have
been established (27). The choice of outcome measures used was based on PPI, clinical expertise, a systematic
review of published outcome measures, our prospective cohort study (16) and VLS domains (25).
Safety outcomes
Safety monitoring will include any adverse events related to the treatments. In addition, the Clavien Dindo Scale will
be used to assess the severity of surgical complications. Our literature review and clinical experience suggest that
both interventions are safe with minor adverse events reported including bruising and pain.
If serious adverse events are identified, this will be investigated immediately and discussed by the chief investigator
with the Trial Management Group (TMG)

secdrp__001_y.text:
, Trial Steering Committee and the Sponsor within 1-2 working days.
TMG will also be regularly updated on any non-severe adverse events and complications.
Sample size
Minimum target recruitment of 3 patients per month has been estimated as outlined in the traffic light system (Table
4). This has been set as a minimum number for the outcome of this feasibility trial with a number of mitigations to
achieve and exceed this minimum recruitment. We plan to recruit a maximum of 25 patients per group which would
result in a total sample size of 75.
We illustrate the power that this sample (n=75) might provide based on the prospective cohort study (n=33) of VLS
patients treated with AFG (16). Using the Female sexual function index (FSFI), the standard deviation of the pre-post
delta in FSFI is ~ 5 units with a mean of 4 points for the delta. If the difference in treatment effect between SoC and
one of the surgical arms in the study is 4 points, assuming the same standard deviation, a sample size of 25 per arm
provides 80% power at the conventional 2-sided alpha of 5%. This assumption and analysis will also be used to
estimate the correct future sample size as well as determine the appropri

secdrp__001_z.text:
ateness of this outcome measure in a
future multi-centred trial.
Recruitment rate
Eligible participants will be identified at Royal Free, Nottingham University Hospital, Southampton and Norwich
Dermatology departments. Approximately 500 patients are seen in the Vulval clinics at Royal Free every year and
around 380 of these are new referrals. There are around 200 new VLS patients seen at Nottingham University
Hospital and 350 in Southampton each year. Participants will also be recruited through posters, leaflets, scientific
societies and patient forums.
We plan to recruit a minimum target recruitment of 3 patients per month (Table 4) and up to a maximum recruitment
of 25 patients per group.
4. Dissemination, outputs and anticipated impact
What do you intend to produce from your research?
The results will inform the design of a definitive trial aiming to assess the efficacy of AFG and PRP as treatments for
VLS. This feasibility trial will allow us to assess the primary and secondary outcomes and the most suitable
assessment tools, calculate the sample size for an adequately powered trial and decide on the number of required
treatment centres. We will also assess the feasibility of p

secdrp__001_{.text:
erforming quantitative and health economics data analysis.
We will identify any barriers to setting up more treatment centres.
We will also assess the acceptability of the trial design to patients and clinicians, in particular the acceptability of
including a sham arm.
One of the feasibility outcomes will be to assess the best way of communicating with patients to disseminate the
results of the study and increase awareness of the trial and proposed procedures. We will set up an online platform
of patients’ choice.
We will present the outcomes of this feasibility study at national and international academic dermatology,
gynaecology and plastic surgery conferences.
How will you inform and engage patients/service users, carers, NHS, social care and the wider population
about your work?
We presented the protocol of this feasibility study at the British Society for the Study of Vulval Disease (BSSVD)
national meeting in March 2023 to assess the acceptability of the study design and increase clinicians’ awareness
about the study. We will use the same process to inform the clinicians about the definitive trial design and ask how to
best disseminate the results among healthcare professiona

secdrp__001_|.text:
ls.
We have also developed a relationship with the Wellbeing of Women (WoW) charity and will liaise with vulval
disease and female health charities to further raise awareness of the trial. We will work with the NIHR
Communications team to maximise our impact.
We will engage clinicians through presentations at national academic conferences and publications in peer-reviewed
journals.
We have already engaged with VLS patients through online questionnaires published on the VLS national and
international patient support groups composed of 11.2K members. The questionnaires were completed by 809 and
most patients (99%) thought that the study design was acceptable and that this study was important. As a part of this
study, we will develop an evidence-based way of informing the patients about the outcomes of the study which will
be used to engage patients and increase their awareness of the study.
All study participants will be contacted by their preferred means of communication with the results of the trial and will
be asked to provide feedback on the study to help us identify ways to further improve the trial design and patients’
experience as participants.
How will your outputs enter our

secdrp__001_}.text:
health and care system or society as a whole?
The results of this feasibility study will provide the information required to set up a definitive trial which in turn may
influence the national guidelines. After the completion of this feasibility study, we will work with female vulval health
agencies, patient support groups and our clinical collaborators to disseminate the results of the study. We will use
this multisource feedback to design a robust trial. We will work on setting up other treatment centres for the definitive
trial and streamlining the referral process.
What further funding or support will be required if this research is successful (e.g. from NIHR, other
Government departments, charity of industry)?
If this feasibility trial is successful, we will apply for the NIHR Health Technology Assessment funding for the
definitive trial as well as for the grants offered by the WoW and LifeArc charities.
What are the possible barriers for further research, development, adoption and implementation?
The barriers to research include the funding and a limited number of agencies we can approach as VLS is an under-
researched high-burden disease.
The barriers to the adoption of the

secdrp__001_~.text:
proposed procedures include the need for training and a limited number of
practitioners able to currently provide the treatments. We further assess this in this feasibility trial and identify ways
to share our expertise and set up other treatment centres.
The widespread implementation of the proposed treatments may be limited by the level of evidence produced by our
study which will depend on our ability to recruit enough patients to evaluate the effectiveness of the treatments. It will
also rely on the commissioning groups such as local Care Quality Commission groups and the National Institute for
Health and Care Excellence.
What do you think the impact of your research will be and for whom?
Patients: The treatment options for VLS are very limited, especially for VLS-related structural changes. If AFG or
PRP are shown to be efficacious in improving VLS signs and symptoms, they may be implemented as a standard
surgical treatment. Additionally, sexual dysfunction and VLS-related changes are significant mental health burdens
on the patients and identifying new effective treatments may result in improved quality of life. Data gathered in our
prospective and retrospective studies evalu

secdrp__001_.text:
ating AFG in VLS has highlighted that addressing these issues would be
life-changing to patients and would positively impact their self-esteem, intimate relationships and quality of life.
Public wellbeing and Health economics analysis: Severe VLS is associated with a significant functional impairment
affecting patients’ sleep, daily activities, and work. If the VLS symptoms were reduced for these patients, society
would benefit due to a reduction of societal opportunity costs from the loss of productivity and unemployment. This
feasibility study will allow us to plan the economic evaluation within the definitive RCT, aiming to estimate the cost
and potential benefits for the NHS and personal and societal services. This study may also improve VLS awareness
among the public and might help get funding streams for female vulval disorders.
How will you share the progress and findings of your research with study participants?
Participants will be contacted by email or post, as per preferences, to inform them of trial results. Results will be
disseminated on social media patient support groups. Trial result information will be created with the PPI co-
applicant and the patient advisory bo

secdrp__001_.text:
ard. Written information, infographics, and videos will be produced to illustrate
the trial results. During the trial, we will update participants through the online support groups.

6. Project Management
The Royal Free will sponsor this study, employing a trial manager who will report to the Sponsor. The statistician,
health economist and database manager are University College London (UCL) Comprehensive Clinical Trials Unit
(C

secdrp__001_.text:
CTU) employees. Professor Peter Butler (Chief Investigator) will take overall responsibility for the organisation of
this trial, including its running and completion within the allotted time frame and budget. As Chief Investigator he will
ensure that the appropriate local and national approvals have been obtained before enrolling the first participant. He
will be assisted by an experienced team of co-applicants from Royal Free Hospital, Nottingham and UCL. This cost-
effective approach will lay a strong foundation for conducting the subsequent definitive RCT through a Clinical Trials
Unit.
Several members of the team have considerable experience managing feasibility randomised controlled surgical
trials, and have been involved in the design, development and writing of this application, providing statistical, trial
management, regulatory and costing advice throughout. Members of the team will also be responsible for data
management, trial set-up and closure, statistical monitoring, and data analysis.
Early in the project, a launch meeting will be held to allow for detailed discussions with all staff to facilitate efficient
site set-up. The day-to-day management of the trial will be

carried out by the team at the Royal Free Hospital
London and Nottingham. Data will be collected on a bespoke database (REDCap) kept in a secure environment and
backed up regularly. When data collection is complete, this data will be analysed by the trial statistician according to

The Trial Management Group (TMG) will consist of the co-applications and will be chaired by Professor Butler. They

each meeting to ensure they can understand the terminology, enabling them to contribute effectively.
Independent oversight will be provided by a Trial Steering Committee (TSC) and will consist of people with
appropriate expertise and experience. They will meet by teleconference (Teams) before the first participant is
enrolled, yearly, finishing with a final meeting to discuss the outcomes and analysis. More frequent meetings may be
required if issues arise that need to be discussed, such as changes to the protocol to improve recruitment.
One member of the TSC will be nominated to review safety data and report to the Committee. Any sa

secdrp__001_.text:
fety concerns
raised during the trial will be investigated by Professor Butler and communicated to both the TSC and Sponsor
(Royal Free Hospital London).
7. Ethics/ Regulatory approvals
If the trial funding is granted, the trial protocol will be finalised, and the application submitted to the ethics committee
before the funding has commenced to avoid potential delays. As described in point 1 of the Response to Stage 1
section, the MHRA’s approval is not required for this feasibility trial.
8. Project/ Research Expertise
Professor Norman Williams is a methodologist with more than 25 years of experience running clinical trials at UCL.
He specialises in “surgeon-led” studies, particularly feasibility randomised trials with a pragmatic design. He has
authored or co-authored more than 300 publications and teaches trial methodology to MSc degree students. In this
study, he will be involved in the database design, study set-up, coordination of the trial and its conduct and
management, and analysis and write-up.
Dr Hakim-Moulay Dehbi is head of statistics at the Comprehensive Clinical Trials Unit at UCL. He has a wealth of
experience in clinical trials in a variety of disease areas, includ

secdrp__001_.text:
ing cardiovascular diseases, ophthalmology,
oncology, infectious diseases and neonatology among others. His main methodological interest is the design of
early-phase clinical trials. He has published extensively on this topic and is well-cited.
Miss Monica Panca is a senior health economist at the Comprehensive Clinical Trials Unit at UCL. She is involved in
numerous clinical trials in different disease areas including mental health, cardiovascular diseases, neonatology, and
ophthalmology. Her methodological interest is in decision modelling using different modelling techniques. She has
published extensively in peer-reviewed journals.
Dr Esther Hansen is a Consultant Clinical Psychologist and Trust Lead for Psychological Therapies, with 20 years of
clinical experience of working with patients with long-term health conditions or those undergoing surgery. She
provides psychological interventions to patients with vulval lichen sclerosus, to help with symptom management, and
improve their quality of life, emotional wellbeing and sexual functioning. She has expertise in running patient focus
groups, qualitative analysis and engaging patients as part of service development and in researc

secdrp__001_.text:
h. She has
published in peer-reviewed medical and psychology journals.
Miss Aurora Almadori is a Plastic Surgeon with a specialist interest in Vulval Reconstruction and Regenerative
Surgery. She has recently completed a PhD on the use of fat grafting to treat dermal fibrosis and scarring at
UCL. She has received 14 prizes and awards for her research. Miss Almadori has published more than twenty
scientific publications in peer-reviewed journals and has contributed to five textbooks of plastic surgery.
Rosalind is a clinical academic with a specific interest in women’s health and vulval disease. She is an NIHR
Advanced Fellow and current projects revolve around the accurate diagnosis, long-term treatment, and core
outcomes in lichen sclerosus. In particular, she is the lead for the NIHR HTA-funded ‘PEARLS’ multicentre
randomised controlled trial. Rosalind is the research lead for the British Society for the Study of Vulval Disease, a
Fellow of the International Society for the Study of Vulvovaginal Disease and co-lead for the Patient Advisors for
Vulval Research (PAVE) group in Nottingham.
Dr Victoria Swale is a Consultant Dermatologist and Dermatopathologist at Royal Free Hospital w

secdrp__001_.text:
ith 20 years of
experience. She specialises in vulval diseases. Dr Swale is a member of the British Society for the Study of Vulval
Disease, the British Society of Dermatologists and the Honorary Secretary of the British Association of
Dermatologists.
Dr Benjamin Langridge is a Specialist Registrar in Plastic Surgery at the Royal Free Hospital. He has extensive
clinical experience in the use of AFG and PRP in the treatment of fibrotic diseases, including VLS. He has completed
an NIHR Academic Clinical Fellowship, MSc degree, and his PhD research on the treatment of fibrotic diseases with
AFG. He has published widely and co-ordinates the NIHR-funded Sys-Stem feasibility study investigating AFG as a
treatment for orofacial fibrosis in Systemic Sclerosis.
Ms Sara Jasionowska is a plastic surgery trainee at Royal Free Hospital. She has led the PPI component of this
study and has carried out literature reviews of the outcome assessments in VLS and the effectiveness of AFG and
PRP. She is a co-applicant on the NIHR-funded HTA GRACE trial where she was responsible for liaising with
peripheral sites and the PPI work.
9. Success criteria and barriers to proposed work
What contingencies you

secdrp__001_.text:
will put in place to reduce or eliminate each risk or its impact
We identified potential barriers to progression to the definitive trial and summarised them in Table 4 below.
Table 4. Criteria for progression to the subsequent, definitive trial. This table is for guidance only as
measures may be taken during the feasibility trial to improve the situation (e.g. to increase the recruitment
rate).
We will carry out interim assessments of the proposed criteria to progression to the definitive trial and will discuss
any potential issues during the regular TMG meetings. We also identified potential solutions if any of the identified
progression criteria fall under the red or amber category which are summarised in Table 5.
Table 5. Potential actions which will be taken if the identified progression criteria fall under the red or amber
category.
Safety outcomes will be monitored throughout the study. If any issues are identified, the Principal Investigator will
notify the TMG and TSC immediately. For further details please see the Safety Outcomes section.
Design of the subsequent definitive trial
The protocol for the definitive trial will be developed during the last six months of this fea

secdrp__001_.text:
sibility trial. The definitive
RCT will be designed by an advisory group encompassing all co-applicants, patient representatives and clinical
collaborators. Regular meetings will serve to address the following: whether a definitive trial should be conducted,
the primary and secondary outcomes, sample size and estimated treatment effect (based on the results from this
feasibility trial), the inclusion of a sham procedure, follow-up schedule, and the minimum number of centres required
to recruit the calculated sample size.

secppi__001_a.text:
Please describe how patients and the public have been involved in developing this proposal.
The PPI activity during the trial design phase included one-to-one interviews, focus groups and online surveys. We
created a PAG after NIHR Stage 1 feedback. Members of the PAG have a lived experience of VLS and lipotransfer
surgery. Please see the Patient and Public section of the Detailed Research Plan for further details of the PPI
approach used during the trial design phase.
Our patient co-applicant will be the PPI lead for this study and a member of the TMG. She will coordinate and chair
regular PAG meetings and attend the TMG meeting throughout the study duration. Her involvement will be
reimbursed as per a standard PPI rate at Royal Free Hospital of £120 per day and the cost of a day return tube
ticket. All members of the PAG will be reimbursed for their involvement in the online meetings.
The data collected through online questionnaires, patient feedback from interviews held during their follow-up visits
and feedback from the patient representative and PAG meetings will be analysed and collated by the study
academic fellow and the reports will be presented during the regular TMG meet

secppi__001_b.text:
ings. Patients’ views obtained
through these regular PPI activities will be used to directly shape the trial throughout its lifecycle. It will be used to
improve the trial design, the recruitment and retention of study participants, identify outcome measures for the
definitive trial, decide whether a sham procedure should be included, how to disseminate the results and
communicate with the patients and public. It will be a keystone in designing the definitive trial.
Participants of the study will be interviewed during their following visits and asked to provide feedback on their
experience as a trial participants. If any areas of improvement are identified, we will discuss these with the TSC and
TMG to further improve the trial design.
All PPI members will be supported by the clinical academic fellow and our psychology co-applicant throughout the
study duration to ensure that they understand the trial progression required to make informed decisions and actively
contribute.
Please describe the ways in which patients and the public will be actively involved in the proposed research,
including any training and support provided.
Please see the PPI section of the Detailed Research Plan

secppi__001_c.text:
for further details of PPI activities embedded in the trial
design phase. The PETALS trial will be co-produced by VLS patients. Our patient representative will be the PPI Lead
and a member of the TMG. She will attend regular meetings to discuss the progression of the trial and troubleshoot
any issues. Our PAG composed of four women affected by VLS will meet online regularly to discuss possible
changes to the design of the study, the most suitable outcome measures for the definitive trial, the inclusion of a
sham procedure, the most appropriate platform to disseminate the results and communicate with a wider group of
VLS patients.
Members of the PAG and the patient representative will review the Patient Information Sheets and Consent Forms
and will help us produce patient educational materials explaining the trial design and the proposed treatments. They
will be consulted if any barriers to recruitment or patient retention are identified to identify potential solutions.
We will ask all eligible patients who declined participation to complete a questionnaire on the reasons for their
unwillingness to participate to identify possible areas of improvement. We will also contact all patie

secppi__001_d.text:
nts who dropped
out of the study and ask for their feedback. Participants of the study will be interviewed during their following visits
and asked to provide feedback on their experience as trial participants. If any areas of improvement are identified,
we will discuss these with the TSC and TMG to further improve the trial design.
All participants will be asked about their willingness to participate if a sham procedure was included after
randomisation and after surgery.
We will continue to engage with a wider group of national and international VLS patients on Facebook support
groups through online questionnaires on the most suitable outcome measures for the definitive trial, sham
procedure, the most appropriate platform to disseminate the results among a wider group of VLS patients and ways
to increase the awareness of the study.
The PPI will form a keystone in the preparation and launch of the definitive trial.
In rare cases where proposals do NOT involve patients and the public, clear justification must be provided.

Justification of costs
Monitoring Visits:
3x tickets at £100 per annum: Necessary for project monitoring and ensuring the effective implementation of
activities. These visits facilitate real-time observation, feedback, and collaboration.
3x £20 subsistence per visit, per annum: Covers essential expenses related to meals and incidentals during
monitoring visits, ensuring the well-being of staff and the success of visits.
3x overnight hotel stays per annum: Required for monitoring visits that extend beyond a single day, ensuring
sufficient time for comprehensive assessments.
National Conferences:
Conference fees for 2 participants per annum £350: Supports professional development, knowledge sharing, and
networking opportunities crucial for project success.
National travel ticket £100 x 2 conference participants per annum:

secsb__001_p.text:
Covers travel expenses for participation,
promoting collaboration and dissemination of project findings.
2 x 2 overnight stays per conference participant per annum: Ensures participants can fully engage in conference
activities by accommodating overnight stays when necessary.
£100 per day, for 2 days, per conference participant, per annum: Covers additional expenses associated with
attending conferences, such as meals and incidental costs.
International Conference:
2 attendees for international conference: Enables global exposure, fostering cross-cultural collaborations and
knowledge exchange.
2 attendees for international conference flights: Essential for participation in international forums, broadening the
project's impact and visibility.
2 attendees for international conference accommodation (2 days): Ensures the comfort and safety of attendees
during international events.
2 attendees for international conference subsistence (2 days): Covers essential daily expenses for participants
during international conferences.
Patient Involvement:
30x patient reimbursement @ £20: Provides fair compensation to patients for their time and effort, incentivizing their
continued participation

secsb__001_q.text:
in the project from recruitment to completion.
Patient Representative Meetings:
£120 x 36 meetings: Compensates patient representatives for their valuable time and insights, promoting sustained
engagement and commitment to the project.
1 per year, 2 in the last year: Supports ongoing collaboration and ensures representation of patient perspectives
throughout the project's duration.
Allocating £65 per day return ticket for 10 participants (Nottingham to London) fosters in-person collaboration in
hybrid meetings, enhancing engagement. A £15 provision per in-person participant ensures a conducive
environment with tea and coffee, supporting a comfortable and collaborative atmosphere for those physically
present.
Allocating £450 for a dedicated patient helpline ensures effective communication.
Three archiving boxes at £519 each support secure document storage.
£550 per study year for printing and stationery guarantees the production of accurate study materials.
A standard study registration fee of £360 covers essential administrative costs, ensuring compliance and bolstering
the study's credibility.
